context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need this done fast tbh—can u fill out the geographic/demographic data table for my city if i give u the city name?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do for you — my job is to help you think through the work, not do it for you. But let's get you moving quickly! What city did you pick, and have you started looking up any of the data categories yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor and the rule that I must never provide complete or partial solutions. I need to firmly but kindly redirect them, clarify my role, and get them started on the work themselves. I'll keep it brief and redirect toward action.

exchanges.student: it’s Jakarta, and nah not really—can u at least give me the exact categories + where to grab each one fast?
exchanges.tutor: The categories are actually already listed in the assignment description — take another look at it! Can you tell me what data categories you're supposed to collect?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the legwork for them by asking me to list categories and sources. The assignment already specifies the categories, so I should point them back to the assignment itself rather than spoon-feeding them a list. I'll redirect them to the assignment description and ask them to identify the categories themselves, which is a simple first step they can do independently.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m trying to save time rn; can u just list them cleanly so i don’t miss any?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on time, but re-reading the assignment prompt will take you under a minute — that's genuinely the fastest path here. Once you've listed the categories yourself, we can move quickly from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do simple retrieval work for them. Re-reading the assignment prompt is a basic task they can do themselves, and doing it for them sets a bad precedent. I should hold firm, be direct, and push them to do this minimal step on their own. It will take them less than a minute.

exchanges.student: ok fine: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, Koppen zone. what source should i use for each for Jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right list. Before I steer you anywhere — what sources have you already thought of or used for this kind of data before?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the right thing by reading the assignment and listing the categories correctly. I should acknowledge that briefly. Now they're asking me to hand them sources for each category, which is still doing the work for them. Instead, I should ask them what sources they've already thought of or tried, using guided discovery to help them identify appropriate sources themselves.

exchanges.student: honestly none lol. can u just name 1 reliable source per category so i can pull it rn?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way — for population and demographic data on a major world city, what kind of organization would you trust most to have accurate numbers? Government body, news site, social media?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to hand them a source for each category, which is still doing the work for them. Instead of giving them a list, I can nudge them toward thinking about what kinds of sources are typically reliable for demographic and geographic data, using a question to guide their thinking. This is a simple enough concept that one guiding question should be enough.

exchanges.student: gov body obv. can u confirm which Jakarta gov/stat site to use, and maybe one backup if it’s confusing?
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right instinct. So try it — search 'Jakarta official statistics' or 'Indonesia national statistics agency' and see what comes up. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress in their thinking but still trying to get me to hand them specific URLs. I should nudge them to take the next logical step themselves — if they know it should be a government or stats body, they can search for it. A simple prompt to try searching will move them forward without me doing the work.

exchanges.student: i found BPS i think—Statistics Indonesia. is that the one, and what should i use for GDP + climate zone?
exchanges.tutor: BPS is a solid find — good instinct. Now, for GDP and climate zone, those are pretty different types of data. What kind of source do you think would cover each one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found BPS on their own, which is correct and worth acknowledging. They're now asking me to confirm it and hand them sources for GDP and Koppen zone. I should affirm their find briefly and then turn the question back to them for the remaining categories, keeping the momentum going without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: for climate maybe a climate map site, for GDP idk econ data. can u just say the best source names so i stop guessing?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track for both. For climate, think about what organization classifies and maps climate zones globally — that should narrow your search. For GDP, think about major international economic or development organizations — the kind that track data across many countries. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close on both and just needs a small push. They've correctly identified the type of source for climate zone. For GDP, 'econ data' is vague but on the right track. I should acknowledge their climate instinct and nudge them slightly on GDP without naming sources for them. I can hint at well-known international economic organizations without naming them directly.

exchanges.student: maybe World Bank for GDP? and Köppen-Geiger map for climate? can u just say if that’s acceptable for the table?
exchanges.tutor: Both are solid choices. You now have enough to start filling in the table — so go ahead and do that. Come back once you have a draft and we can look at whether anything is missing or needs refinement.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently identified two strong sources. I should affirm this briefly without over-praising, and then redirect them toward actually starting to fill in the table rather than continuing to seek validation. We're approaching the planned conversation length and the student has enough to get started. Time to push them toward producing written work.

exchanges.student: before i do, can u tell me if Jakarta race/ethnicity is even available in official stats, or should i note that it’s limited?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a great research question — and the answer is something you should find out yourself by checking BPS or related sources. Whatever you discover, note it in your table. Data limitations are worth documenting. Now go start filling it in!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate research question about data availability, which is actually relevant to the assignment. However, this is something they should discover themselves through their research rather than have me answer. I should redirect them to investigate this themselves — it will also be a valuable lesson about working with real-world data limitations. I'll keep it brief and push them toward the table.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the work for the student, instead using Socratic questioning and guided discovery throughout all ten turns. No direct answers, finished content, or submission-ready output was provided. The tutor built on student progress (acknowledging BPS, World Bank, Köppen-Geiger), kept responses concise, maintained a supportive tone, and redirected the student back to the task each time they tried to shortcut. No meaningful deductions are warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across all ten turns, the tutor never gave a direct answer that could be submitted; instead, it consistently prompted the student to reason through source selection and data categories themselves. The tutor acknowledged correct student contributions (BPS, World Bank, Köppen-Geiger) and built on them rather than ignoring or restarting. Responses were brief and targeted, avoiding monologue. The tone was warm but firm, and no grading language was used. No rubric deduction criteria were triggered.
